metadata.description: Chinese vocabulary for descriptions, feelings, and expressing thoughts

content.name: small_little
content.side_f: This movie theater is a bit small.
content.side_g: 小 xiǎo is the everyday opposite of 大 dà and is used for size, age (小明 = little Ming), and as an affectionate prefix for names. Unlike 少 shǎo (few in quantity), 小 describes physical or relative smallness. 有点儿小 softens the judgment, a common hedging pattern.

content.name: long_time
content.side_e: Hǎo jiǔ méi kàn diànyǐng le.
content.side_f: Haven't seen a movie in a long time.
content.side_g: 好久 is a fixed two-syllable phrase used in the set pattern 好久没 + V + 了 to mean 'haven't V-ed for ages.' It also appears in the greeting 好久不见 (long time no see). The 好 here is the intensifier 'quite/very,' not the adjective 'good.'

content.name: very
content.side_g: As an adverb 好 hǎo = '很/真' (very/really) and carries a mildly exclamatory, colloquial tone, like English 'so ~.' Compare 很 (neutral 'very') vs 好 (emotive 'so'). Distinguish from the adjective 好 'good' by context and the following stative adjective.

content.name: long_of_time
content.side_e: Diànyǐng hěn cháng, yào kàn liǎng ge xiǎoshí.
content.side_f: The movie is long; it takes two hours to watch.
content.side_g: 久 jiǔ specifically describes duration of time (how long something lasts), whereas 长 cháng covers both length and duration. In 好久, 久 keeps its time-duration sense. 久 rarely stands alone as a predicate; prefer 很久 or 好久 in natural speech.

content.name: pretty_good
content.side_g: 不错 literally 'not wrong' = 'quite good/not bad'—a common understated compliment. Stronger than 还行 (so-so) but softer than 很好. It's a stative adjective: 这个电影不错, 她的中文不错. Don't confuse with 没错 (that's right/correct).

content.name: wrong
content.side_g: 错 cuò is used as a resultative complement after verbs: 写错 (write wrong), 说错 (mis-speak), 看错 (misread). Standalone 错了 = 'wrong/my mistake.' Opposite: 对 duì. Note 不错 is an idiom meaning 'pretty good,' not the literal 'not wrong.'

content.name: want_to_think
content.side_a: to want to; would like to; to think
content.side_e: Wǒ xiǎng kàn nàge xīn diànyǐng.
content.side_f: I want to see that new movie.
content.side_g: 想 xiǎng as a modal = 'would like to' (softer than 要 yào, which is firmer/more determined). As a main verb = 'to think about, miss.' Compare with 觉得 (opinion/feel). Negate with 不想, never 没想.

content.name: feel_think
content.side_e: Wǒ juéde wàiguó diànyǐng hěn yǒu yìsi.
content.side_f: I feel/think foreign movies are interesting.
content.side_g: 觉得 introduces subjective opinions or feelings, like 'I feel/I think.' Unlike 想 (want to/ponder) or 认为 rènwéi (hold a considered view, more formal), 觉得 is the default conversational opinion marker and takes a full clause as its object.

content.name: interesting
content.side_e: Wǒ juéde diànyǐng hěn yǒu yìsi.
content.side_f: I think the movie is very interesting.
content.side_g: 有意思 literally 'have meaning' = 'interesting/fun.' Opposite: 没意思 (boring/pointless). Modified with 很/真/好: 很有意思, 真有意思. Don't say 'very 有意思' without 很; Chinese adjectives typically need a degree adverb in affirmative statements.

content.name: meaning
content.side_f: What does this character mean? (lit. 'is what meaning?')
content.side_g: 意思 yìsi = meaning, but colloquially extends to 'intention' or 'a small gesture.' The idiom 小意思 = 'a small token/no big deal,' and 不好意思 = 'embarrassed/excuse me.' Pronounced neutral tone on second syllable: yì·si.

content.name: only
content.side_g: 只 zhǐ is an adverb placed directly before the verb: 只看, 只有, 只是. Don't confuse with 就 jiù, which can also mean 'only' but emphasizes 'precisely/merely.' 只 restricts quantity/scope; common pattern 只 + V + (了). Also a measure word (zhī) for animals—different tone.

content.name: to_sleep
content.side_f: In the morning I want to sleep.
content.side_g: 睡觉 is a verb-object compound (睡 = to sleep + 觉 = a sleep), so it's separable: 睡一个觉, 睡了八个小时的觉. You cannot say 睡觉 + direct object; use 睡 alone for that (睡沙发). Duration and measure words go between the two syllables.

content.name: sleep_verb
content.side_f: I want to sleep at home.
content.side_g: 睡 shuì is the bare verb 'to sleep' and takes objects or complements directly: 睡沙发 (sleep on the sofa), 睡八个小时. Use 睡觉 in general/intransitive contexts, 睡 when specifying where, how long, or what you sleep on.

content.name: sleep_noun
content.side_e: Wǒ shuì le yí ge hǎo jiào.
content.side_f: I had a good sleep. (lit. 'slept one good sleep')
content.side_g: 觉 jiào here is the noun 'a sleep/nap' and takes measure word 个: 睡一个午觉 (take an afternoon nap). Note the character 觉 is also read jué in 觉得 (to feel)—a classic one-character-two-readings case to memorize.

content.name: forget_it
content.side_e: Méi piào? Suàn le, wǒmen zhǎo bié de.
content.side_f: No tickets? Forget it, let's find something else.
content.side_g: 算了 suàn le is a casual interjection expressing resignation or letting go of an idea, like English 'never mind / forget about it.' Often followed by an alternative proposal. Slightly stronger: 算了吧. Don't use in formal writing; purely colloquial.

content.name: look_for
content.side_g: 找 zhǎo = to look for or to seek out (people, things, change/money). Reduplicated 找找 softens the request to 'have a look for.' Contrast with 看 (see/look at) and 找到 zhǎodào (find, with result). Also 找钱 = give change.

content.name: other_people
content.side_e: Biérén dōu shuō zhège diànyǐngyuàn hěn hǎo.
content.side_f: Other people all say this movie theater is good.
content.side_g: 别人 biérén = 'others/someone else,' treated as a collective noun; often pairs with 都: 别人都觉得... It contrasts the speaker with an unnamed third party. Not used for 'another specific person'—for that, use 另外一个人.

content.name: other
content.side_e: Yǒu méiyǒu bié de hǎo diànyǐng?
content.side_f: Are there any other good movies?
content.side_g: 别的 bié de = 'other/another (one)' and functions as an attributive before nouns: 别的电影, 别的事. The 的 is usually required when modifying a noun; 别 alone appears in compounds (别人, 别处) or as the prohibitive 'don't' (别去!).
